name: Finite State Machine Architecture for Onboarding

fields.definition_origin: Finite State Machines (FSM) formalized by Mealy (1955) and Moore (1956) for digital circuits; David Harel's 'Statecharts: A Visual Formalism for Complex Systems' (Sci. Comput. Program. 1987) extended FSMs with hierarchy, parallelism, and history — basis for modern software FSMs. XState by David Khourshid (2017, now Stately.ai) implemented Statecharts in TypeScript and is the de-facto industry standard for app FSMs in 2024-2026; XState v5 released 2024 introduced first-class TypeScript inference, persistence helpers (waitFor, persistedState), and actor model. BLoC pattern (Felix Angelov, 2019) is Flutter's parallel — also FSM-derived. iOS 'Manager-of-Flows' / 'Coordinator pattern' (Soroush Khanlou 2015, Khanlou.com) decomposes navigation into a hierarchy of state-machine-like coordinators. The PDF 'Strategic Architecture and Psychological Frameworks for Mobile Application Onboarding' (.agent/Deep Dive Into App Onboarding.pdf) explicitly states: 'onboarding is not a sequence of views — it is a state machine.'
fields.core_mechanism: An FSM models the onboarding flow as a finite set of mutually-exclusive states (idle, asking_question, loading_plan, paywall_visible, completed) connected by named events (NEXT, BACK, SUBMIT, FAILURE). Transitions are pure functions of (current_state, event, context) → (next_state, side_effects). This solves the canonical onboarding bug class: imperative navigation logic scattered across screen components, where 'should the back button work here?' / 'what if network drops mid-step?' / 'can the user be on step 5 if they haven't completed step 3?' become impossible to reason about as the flow grows. FSM centralizes the truth into one transition table, makes invalid states unrepresentable (you literally cannot be in two states at once), enables fault-tolerance via persistence (serialize state, restore on relaunch — item 32), and permits property-based testing (every state, every event, every transition can be enumerated and tested). Statecharts (XState) extend with hierarchical states (compound state machines — onboarding > section > question), parallel regions (auth state independent from questionnaire state), guards (predicates that allow/deny transitions), and actions (named side effects). The conceptual frame: a screen renders FROM state, never decides state.
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) XState v5 is widely adopted: stately.ai reports 5M+ npm weekly downloads on @xstate/* packages (2024), used by Microsoft, Vercel, Apollo, BlueSky, Airbnb in production. (2) David Khourshid's talks at React Conf 2018-2024, Frontend Masters 'State Machines in Front-End Development' course. (3) Stripe Atlas, Notion, Linear all describe state-machine-driven flows in engineering blogs. (4) PDF deep-dive: explicit statement that mobile onboarding succeeds when treated as FSM, not view sequence. (5) Mobile-specific: AsyncStorage / MMKV state-persistence patterns documented in React Native Reanimated/RN core docs assume FSM-shaped state. (6) BLoC pattern usage in Flutter onboarding apps documented in Google Flutter samples (e.g., Reply, Wonderous, Material gallery). (7) Empirical ROI difficult to isolate — FSM is an architecture choice that improves maintainability and testability; conversion-lift evidence is indirect (fewer bugs → fewer mid-flow crashes → higher completion). Stately.ai customer case studies cite reduced flow-bugs by 60-80% post-FSM-adoption (anecdotal, vendor-published).
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Mid-flow crash rate, navigation-bug count per release, time-to-onboarding-A/B-test (faster with FSM + server-driven config), state-corruption incidents (impossible-state defects), automated test coverage of flow paths (FSM enables 100% transition coverage via property-based testing), engineer hours per onboarding-flow change.
fields.evidence_strength: industry_benchmark + practitioner_opinion (architectural pattern; conversion lift indirect)
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Indirect — FSM reduces flow bugs which reduces mid-step abandonment; specific lift not measurable in isolation. Stately.ai vendor-cited 60-80% bug reduction post-adoption (n=anecdotal customer reports).
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Microsoft onboarding flows in Microsoft Learn use XState (cited in Stately customer page). (2) BlueSky onboarding (account creation, invite-code flow, profile setup) uses XState v5 (open-source, github.com/bluesky-social/social-app). (3) Linear's app uses state machines for invite flows (Linear engineering blog). (4) Vercel's deployment dashboard onboarding (XState reference customer). (5) Stripe Atlas onboarding (mentioned by David K. as state-machine-driven). (6) Apollo Client's request lifecycle is FSM. (7) Flutter Reply demo app uses BLoC for onboarding screens. (8) Khanlou's Coordinator pattern adopted by Trafi, Hopin, several iOS-first startups. (9) Cal AI's 33-step onboarding uses unidirectional reducer-based state (Redux-class) — functionally an FSM though not advertised as such. (10) Adapty's Onboarding Builder (2026) emits a JSON-defined state machine consumed by their SDK at runtime — server-driven FSM commercial product.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: FSM concept identical; tooling differs slightly. Mobile (RN/Expo): @xstate/react + react-native-mmkv for persistence + react-navigation for screen rendering driven by state. iOS native: SwiftUI + Combine + custom state-machine reducer, or Composable Architecture (TCA) which is FSM-derived (Point-Free). Android native: Kotlin Coroutines + sealed-class state hierarchies + Compose, or Orbit MVI (Babylon Health). Web: same XState. Mobile-specific concerns: background termination (state must persist), navigation (back-button must integrate with FSM event), deep-link entry (AD click → onboarding variant — must enter machine at correct state), push-notification re-entry. Web equivalent: same persistence to localStorage/IndexedDB, same browser-back integration.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: N/A — architectural, not visual. FSM is a pure-data layer; UI rendering remains free to use Liquid Glass / M3E primitives.
fields.category_fit: Strongest for: long onboarding (>5 steps), multi-branching flows (paywall → soft-paywall → win-back → completed), apps requiring fault-tolerant resume (item 32, 33), apps with A/B-test heavy iteration, B2B with role-based onboarding branches. Acceptable for: short flows (4-screen cinematic — item 25 — FSM is overkill but harmless). Weak fit: trivial 1-2 screen flows where useState/useReducer is enough. NOT recommended to skip FSM for any flow with persistence needs.
fields.solo_dev_feasibility: 1_week. XState v5 + @xstate/react learning curve 2-3 days; implement 12-step onboarding machine 1-2 days; persistence integration 1 day; tests another day. Existing onboarding-as-state-machine boilerplates (OnboardJS) reduce to 2-3 days for a solo dev.
fields.tooling_options: PRIMARY: XState v5 (stately.ai/docs) — TS-first, persistence helpers (`getPersistedSnapshot`, `createActor({snapshot})`), actor model, parallel/hierarchical states, visual editor. ALTERNATIVES: Robot (smaller XState alternative, Matthew Phillips), Zustand + custom reducer (lighter, no formal FSM but common pattern), Redux Toolkit + RTK Query (heavier, mature). MOBILE NATIVE: Composable Architecture / TCA (iOS, Point-Free), Orbit MVI (Android), BLoC (Flutter, flutter_bloc). VISUAL EDITOR: Stately.ai Studio (web-based, exports machine code). TESTING: @xstate/test (model-based testing), @xstate/graph (path generation). SERVER-DRIVEN: Adapty Onboarding Builder, Plotline (both emit FSM JSON). FREE TIER: XState OSS unlimited; Stately Studio free up to 3 machines.
fields.ai_dependency: none for the FSM itself. Optional: AI-driven plan-fetch invoked from a state (loadingPlan → fetchPersonalizedPlan actor) layers LLM call on top.
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude to define the machine FIRST, components second. Prompt fragment 1: 'Implement onboarding as an XState v5 machine. Define type Context = { step, totalSteps, answers, archetype, locale, planId, error }. Define type Events = { ANSWER, NEXT, BACK, SKIP, PAYWALL_DISMISSED, PAYWALL_PURCHASED, RESUME }. States: questionnaire, loadingPlan (invokes fetchPersonalizedPlan actor), planError, paywall, softPaywall, completed (final). Use guards: isLastStep, hasMinimumAnswers. Actions: advance, rewind, persistAnswer, persistToDisk (MMKV.set), emitTelemetry. Output the machine FIRST as a complete TypeScript file, then implement screens that subscribe via useSelector. FORBID: imperative navigation logic in screen components. FORBID: useState for onboarding step counter — state lives in the machine. FORBID: react-navigation push/pop scattered across files — navigation reacts to state.value.' Prompt fragment 2: 'Add persistence: on every transition, persist `actorRef.getPersistedSnapshot()` to MMKV. On app launch, hydrate via `createActor(machine, { snapshot: MMKV.getString(KEY) ? JSON.parse(...) : undefined })`. Add a RESUME event for cross-device hydration from server. Output property-based tests using @xstate/test that cover every state-event transition.'
fields.stitch_application: FSM is non-visual; Stitch instructions focus on rendering screens FROM state. Prompt fragment: 'Each screen renders FROM the onboarding state machine context: question header reads from state.context.answers metadata; progress bar reads (state.context.step + 2) / (state.context.totalSteps + 2). Mockup three screens corresponding to states questionnaire/step=4, loadingPlan, paywall. Show explicitly which context fields are interpolated into each screen.'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI defaults to imperative useState + setState scattered across screens with prop-drilling step counter through navigation params. AI computes step transitions inside onPress handlers, with no central transition table. AI uses react-navigation imperatively (navigation.navigate('Step5')) breaking on back-button. AI doesn't handle network-fail mid-step. AI doesn't persist state across app kill. Counter-prompt: 'FORBID useState for onboarding state — must use XState v5 actor. FORBID imperative navigation.navigate calls — navigation must react to state.value via useEffect on state change. FORBID prop-drilling step counter — use useSelector(actor, s => s.context.step). FORBID local async loading flags inside screens — represent loading as machine state (loadingPlan). Output the machine definition FIRST in a separate file (`onboardingMachine.ts`), then screens. Provide error states (planError) with retry and skip transitions. Persist state via getPersistedSnapshot on every transition.' AI also under-uses guards — counter: 'use guards for all conditional transitions (isLastStep, hasMinimumAnswers, isFirstStep) — never inline conditions in target arrays.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Imperative navigation scattered across screens — bug-class explosion at >5 screens. (2) Local useState for cross-screen step counter — state desyncs on navigation back. (3) No persistence — app kill loses progress (item 32). (4) FSM without guards — invalid transitions silently allowed (e.g., advance without answer). (5) FSM without explicit error/loading states — async failures crash UI. (6) Over-engineered FSM for trivial flows — useReducer suffices for <3 states. (7) FSM events as strings without TS-typed Events union — typo bugs at runtime. (8) Mixing FSM with GetStream/Realm/SQL DB tightly — couples persistence to FSM lifecycle awkwardly; keep separate. (9) Visual designer treats FSM diagram as 'mockup' instead of source of truth — code drift inevitable. (10) Forgetting RESUME / hydration event when state schema changes — version state object, migrate on resume.
fields.regulatory_risk: Low — architectural pattern, not user-facing. Risk surfaces only via what the machine does: if state machine implements dark patterns (no-back-allowed flow, see No-Escape antipattern), DFA Q4 2026 flags. FSM transparency itself is compliance-positive: makes audit-trail of user journey easier (GDPR Art. 30 records of processing).
fields.platform_constraints_2026: No platform restriction. Apple/Google neutral on architecture. State persistence interacts with iOS Background App Refresh (state must serialize before suspend), Android process death (use SavedStateHandle in addition to MMKV).
fields.accessibility_implications: FSM is non-visual but enables better a11y: each state has a single focusable region, deterministic focus management on transitions (announce screen change to VoiceOver/TalkBack via AccessibilityInfo.announceForAccessibility on state.value change). State-driven UI lets you derive accessibilityLabel from state context. Avoid: rendering multiple states' UI simultaneously (parallel regions confusing for AT) — use parallel regions only for independent concerns (auth + questionnaire), not visible UI.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Bundle size — XState v5 is ~25kb gzipped (acceptable, but Robot is ~2kb if budget critical). (2) Learning curve — junior devs new to FSM thinking write awkward machines first 2 weeks. (3) Versioning — state schema changes require migration logic; keep `version` field in context. (4) Server-driven FSM (item 31) requires schema validation on load (untrusted JSON). (5) Persistence frequency — every transition might thrash MMKV on slow devices; debounce 250ms. (6) Test maintenance — property-based tests over-cover (combinatorial explosion); restrict to critical paths. (7) Animation timing during state transitions: spring durations must align with state-change perception (250-400ms transitions). (8) Hot-reload during development can desync persisted state from current schema; clear state on dev-mode schema changes.
fields.prescribed_action: Implement onboarding as an XState v5 actor with explicit Context, Events, States, Guards, Actions. Persist state on every transition via `getPersistedSnapshot()` to MMKV. Hydrate on app launch via `createActor(machine, { snapshot })`. Forbid imperative navigation, useState for cross-screen state, scattered async loading flags. Server-driven UI (item 31) and cross-device hydration (item 33) layer on top of the FSM.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: Indirect but enabling — the questionnaire (items 26, 27, 28) renders FROM state machine context.answers. Each question's ANSWER event mutates context. Step counter (item 29) reads context.step. AI plan reveal (item 12) is a state with invoked actor.
fields.aha_relevance: Indirect — FSM enables fault-tolerant Aha (no lost progress on crash), cross-device Aha (item 33), and reliable theatrical-loader-to-reveal handoff (item 13) via the loadingPlan → paywall transition with guaranteed event delivery.
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: N/A (architectural — but FSM enables archetype-conditioned branches, e.g., a Magician variant of states with different copy/animations vs Caregiver variant).
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Pairs against navigation chaos / impossible-state defects. Pairs with item 32 (Persistence) — FSM state IS the unit of persistence. Pairs with item 33 (Cross-Device Resume) — server stores snapshot, client hydrates via RESUME event. Pairs with item 31 (Server-Driven UI) — FSM definition itself can be JSON-loaded for zero-deploy iteration.
sources: [Statecharts: A Visual Formalism for Complex Systems — David Harel (1987)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0167642387900359)
uncertain: specific conversion lift from FSM adoption (architectural; effect indirect via reduced bugs)
